name: medicine ball chest push with run release
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest level.
Step forward with your right foot and simultaneously push the medicine ball forward, extending your arms fully.
As you push the ball forward, release it and let it roll forward.
Quickly run forward and catch the ball before it hits the ground.
Once you catch the ball, bring it back to your chest and repeat the movement with your left foot forward.
Continue alternating legs and repeating the exercise for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a medicine ball at chest level. Step forward with your right foot and simultaneously push the medicine ball forward, extending your arms fully. As you push the ball forward, release it and let it roll forward. Quickly run forward and catch the ball before it hits the ground. Once you catch the ball, bring it back to your chest and repeat the movement with your left foot forward. Continue alternating legs and repeating the exercise for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
